institution: Sector-wide typology - Indian retail digital payments
category: Account compromise - SIM swap to intercept OTP/SMS second factor
risk_signals: Sudden loss of mobile network / SIM deactivation on the victim's handset
An unexpected 'SIM change' or porting confirmation message
OTPs and transaction alerts stop arriving, followed by unauthorised transfers
analyst_reasoning: If SMS OTP is the second factor, an attacker who obtains a duplicate SIM (via telco social engineering or an insider) receives the victim's OTPs and can reset banking credentials. The loss of signal is the pivot point: the attacker now controls the second factor.
investigation_steps: Build a timeline of the SIM swap/port event against the fraudulent transactions
Obtain telco records on who requested the SIM change and how it was authenticated
Check for new-device logins and beneficiary additions immediately after the swap
decision: Freeze the account, coordinate with the telecom operator to reverse the SIM change, and move the customer to app-based authentication.
outcome: Funds are transferred during the window the attacker controls the SIM; recovery depends on how fast the swap is detected.
lessons_learned: SMS OTP is a weak second factor because the SIM itself can be captured
Loss of mobile signal plus a SIM-change message is an early warning of takeover
App-based or hardware second factors materially reduce SIM-swap exposure
